enrichment.short_description: Lunatask is an end-to-end encrypted task manager, habit tracker, journal, and notes app built specifically for people with ADHD, available on Mac, Windows, Linux, iOS, and Android.
enrichment.seo_title: Lunatask Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lunatask review 2026: an encrypted, ADHD-friendly task manager with habit tracking and journaling. See pricing, features, pros and cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lunatask is an all-in-one personal productivity application that combines a to-do list, habit and mood tracker, journal, notes, and a lightweight personal CRM into a single end-to-end encrypted workspace. It was built by independent developer Carl Pullein and released in 2021 after Pullein, who has ADHD, grew frustrated that mainstream task managers such as Todoist, Things, and OmniFocus were not designed around the way ADHD brains prioritize and get overwhelmed by long lists. Lunatask's core design principle is to show users only what they can act on right now rather than an intimidating wall of tasks, using features like a 'now/next/later' priority system, an Eisenhower Matrix view, and Kanban boards to help users triage work without decision paralysis. Technically, Lunatask stores nearly everything client-side and end-to-end encrypted before it ever reaches Lunatask's sync servers, using the argon2 key-derivation function (implemented in WebAssembly) for password-based key generation and TLS 1.3 for transport security. This means the company itself cannot read a user's tasks, journal entries, habit logs, or notes, which is a differentiator against mainstream competitors that store data in plaintext on their servers. The app ships as native or near-native clients for macOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, and Android, with data syncing across all of them. Beyond task management, Lunatask includes a habit tracker that logs daily habits and mood and surfaces patterns between the two, a private journal with mood and emotion tagging, freeform notes, and a simple relationship/CRM module for keeping track of personal contacts and follow-ups. Tasks can be organized into 'Areas of Life' (for example Work, Health, Family) to give a bird's-eye view of how effort is distributed. Lunatask uses a freemium pricing model. The free tier is usable indefinitely but caps users at 2 Areas of Life, 7 daily habits, and 30 CRM relationships, and does not include calendar integration. The Premium tier unlocks unlimited areas, habits, and relationships, plus calendar integration and all viewing modes, for $8/month billed monthly or $72/year (effectively $6/month) billed annually. Lunatask also offers a one-time lifetime Premium license for $300, an unusual option among modern subscription productivity tools. Lunatask's target audience is individuals, largely knowledge workers, neurodivergent users, and privacy-conscious professionals, rather than teams; it has no team collaboration, shared-workspace, or multi-user administration features, which sets it apart from competitors like Todoist, ClickUp, or Notion. Its closest competitors are other single-user, privacy-focused productivity apps such as TickTick, Things 3, Sunsama, and Amazing Marvin, with Lunatask differentiating itself primarily through its client-side encryption model and explicit ADHD-oriented design language of reduced visual clutter, forced prioritization, and habit-mood correlation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lunatask is a cross-platform personal productivity app that unifies task management, habit tracking, journaling, notes, and a simple personal CRM into one encrypted workspace. It was created by independent developer Carl Pullein and launched in 2021 with a specific focus on users who have ADHD or who feel overwhelmed by traditional, list-heavy to-do apps.

Rather than presenting an endless backlog, Lunatask nudges users toward a small, prioritized set of 'now' tasks using views like the Eisenhower Matrix and Kanban boards, while habit and mood tracking help surface behavioral patterns over time. The app runs natively on macOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, and Android with data synced across devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of Lunatask is its task manager, which lets users group work into 'Areas of Life,' apply priority scoring, and switch between list, Kanban, and Eisenhower Matrix views. A built-in habit tracker logs daily habits and mood, and highlights correlations between the two, while the journaling feature supports private, tagged entries for reflection.

Lunatask's standout feature is its end-to-end encryption: tasks, notes, journal entries, and habit data are encrypted on-device using argon2-derived keys before syncing, so Lunatask's own servers never see readable user data. It also includes a lightweight personal CRM for tracking relationships and follow-ups, and calendar integration on paid plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lunatask follows a freemium model. The free plan has no time limit but restricts users to 2 Areas of Life, 7 daily habits, and 30 CRM relationships, and excludes calendar sync. Premium removes these caps and adds calendar integration for $8/month or $72/year.

Unusually for a modern subscription app, Lunatask also sells a one-time lifetime Premium license for $300, giving budget-conscious or subscription-averse users a way to unlock every feature permanently.

features.title: Encrypted task management
features.description: Tasks, subtasks, and priorities are stored end-to-end encrypted and organized into customizable 'Areas of Life' with list, Kanban, and Eisenhower Matrix views.

features.title: Habit and mood tracking
features.description: Log daily habits and moods, then review charts that surface correlations between specific habits and reported mood or energy levels.

features.title: Private journaling
features.description: Write encrypted journal entries tagged with mood and emotion, kept separate from tasks so reflection does not get lost in a to-do list.

features.title: Personal CRM
features.description: Track relationships and set reminders to follow up with contacts, useful for maintaining personal or professional connections without a dedicated CRM tool.

features.title: Cross-platform sync
features.description: Native apps for macOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, and Android keep tasks, habits, notes, and journal entries synced across every device.

features.title: ADHD-oriented prioritization
features.description: A 'now/next/later' style workflow and forced prioritization limits reduce the overwhelm of long backlog lists, a design choice aimed squarely at ADHD users.

features.title: Calendar integration
features.description: Premium subscribers can connect external calendars to see tasks and events side by side and schedule work around existing commitments.

features.title: Notes
features.description: A dedicated encrypted notes module sits alongside tasks and journal entries for capturing freeform information.

pros: True end-to-end encryption means Lunatask itself cannot read your tasks, journal, or notes.
ADHD-specific design (forced prioritization, reduced clutter) is uncommon among mainstream task managers.
Combines task management, habit tracking, journaling, and a personal CRM in one app instead of several.
One-time lifetime purchase option avoids an open-ended subscription.
Native apps across macOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, and Android.
cons: No team or collaboration features, so it is not suitable for shared work management.
Free plan is fairly limited (2 Areas of Life, 7 habits, 30 CRM contacts, no calendar sync).
Smaller ecosystem and fewer third-party integrations than established players like Todoist or Notion.
Built and maintained by a very small, largely solo team, which can mean slower feature velocity.
Encryption model limits some convenience features, such as server-side search, common in non-encrypted apps.

faqs.question: Is Lunatask free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lunatask has a permanently free tier, but it limits users to 2 Areas of Life, 7 daily habits, and 30 CRM relationships, and it excludes calendar integration. Unlocking all features requires the Premium plan.

faqs.question: How does Lunatask's encryption work?
faqs.answer: Lunatask encrypts tasks, journal entries, notes, and habit data on the user's device using keys derived with the argon2 algorithm before syncing to Lunatask Cloud over TLS 1.3, so the company cannot read stored content.

faqs.question: Does Lunatask support teams?
faqs.answer: No, Lunatask is designed for individual use and does not offer shared workspaces, team task assignment, or multi-user administration.

faqs.question: What platforms does Lunatask run on?
faqs.answer: Lunatask has native apps for macOS, Windows, Linux, iOS, and Android, with data synced across all of them.

faqs.question: Is there a lifetime plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lunatask offers a one-time lifetime Premium purchase for $300 in addition to its $8/month or $72/year subscription.

faqs.question: Who makes Lunatask?
faqs.answer: Lunatask was built by independent developer Carl Pullein, who created the app in 2021 after struggling with ADHD and finding existing task managers overwhelming.

use_cases.title: ADHD-friendly task management
use_cases.description: Individuals with ADHD use Lunatask's forced prioritization and reduced-clutter views to avoid the overwhelm of long backlog lists.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious personal productivity
use_cases.description: Users who do not want their tasks, journal, or notes readable by a vendor choose Lunatask for its end-to-end encryption.

use_cases.title: Habit and mood correlation tracking
use_cases.description: People trying to build habits use Lunatask to log daily habits alongside mood, then review patterns to see what routines actually improve wellbeing.

features: All views including Kanban and Eisenhower Matrix

enrichment.short_description: Lunch Money is a web and mobile personal budgeting app that syncs bank and crypto accounts across multiple currencies, sold on a pay-what-you-want subscription model.
enrichment.starting_price: Free trial, then pay-what-you-want (originally launched at $3/month)
enrichment.seo_title: Lunch Money Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lunch Money review 2026: a pay-what-you-want budgeting app with bank sync, multi-currency support, and shared budgets. Pricing, features, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lunch Money is a web-based, with companion mobile apps, personal finance and budgeting tool that connects to bank accounts, credit cards, investment accounts, and even cryptocurrency wallets to give users a consolidated view of spending, budgets, and net worth. It was founded by Jen Yip, a solo developer who spent roughly a year and a half manually tracking her own multi-currency spending in a spreadsheet while traveling through Europe and Asia during a career break, before turning that spreadsheet into a product. Lunch Money launched publicly in 2019 via Hacker News' Show HN, and for its first several years Yip ran the company alone, handling engineering, design, support, and marketing herself. Lunch Money is bootstrapped and has not raised outside venture funding; it is entirely funded by subscription revenue, which the company has cited as core to its philosophy of building a product for its users rather than for investors. Its signature pricing mechanic is a pay-what-you-want annual subscription: after a free trial, users choose their own price, subject to a minimum, and that price is locked in for as long as they remain subscribed, rewarding early and loyal customers with lower rates than new sign-ups might pay later. The app originally launched at $3/month ($29/year) in 2019; suggested pricing has since risen, though the pay-what-you-want structure means actual prices vary by user and all plans include the full feature set rather than gating features behind tiers. Functionally, Lunch Money aggregates transactions from linked bank and investment accounts via an account-aggregation provider, supports manual account tracking for cash or crypto, and lets users build zero-based or category budgets, tag and split transactions, track recurring bills, and view multi-currency balances converted into a home currency. It supports shared household budgets so partners can track joint finances, and offers a public API and integrations for users who want to build custom automations, appealing to a technically inclined user base. Lunch Money's target audience is individuals and couples who find automated apps too rigid and spreadsheet-based budgeting too manual, particularly freelancers, remote workers, expats, and crypto-curious users who need reliable multi-currency support. Its main competitors include YNAB (You Need A Budget), Copilot Money, Monarch Money, Tiller, and Actual Budget, with Lunch Money differentiating itself through its unusual pay-what-you-want pricing, strong multi-currency and crypto support, and a developer-friendly API, all built and supported by a small, founder-led team rather than a large venture-backed company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lunch Money is a personal finance and budgeting app that connects to bank, credit card, investment, and cryptocurrency accounts to give users a single, multi-currency view of spending and net worth. It was created by founder Jen Yip after she spent a year and a half tracking her own travel spending in a multi-currency spreadsheet, and launched publicly in 2019.

The company is bootstrapped, self-funded entirely by subscriptions, and for its first several years was run by Yip alone. Lunch Money is aimed at people who find automated apps too rigid and spreadsheets too manual, particularly freelancers, remote workers, and users who deal with more than one currency.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lunch Money links to banks and investment accounts through account aggregation, and also supports manually tracked accounts for cash and crypto holdings. Users can build category or zero-based budgets, tag and split transactions, and set up recurring bill tracking.

Multi-currency support is a core differentiator: balances and transactions in different currencies are converted into a home currency for reporting, which is useful for expats, travelers, and crypto holders. Lunch Money also offers shared budgets for couples or households and a public API for custom integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lunch Money uses a pay-what-you-want annual subscription after a free trial: users pick their own price above a minimum, and that price is locked in for as long as they stay subscribed, rather than being tiered by feature.

The app launched in 2019 at $3/month ($29/year); typical suggested pricing has increased since then, but because pricing is user-chosen and locked in, actual cost varies by subscriber, and all subscribers get access to the same full feature set.

features.title: Bank and investment account sync
features.description: Automatically pulls in transactions and balances from linked bank, credit card, and investment accounts.

features.title: Multi-currency support
features.description: Tracks accounts and transactions in different currencies and converts them into a home currency for unified reporting.

features.title: Crypto tracking
features.description: Manually or semi-automatically tracks cryptocurrency holdings alongside traditional accounts.

features.title: Budgeting tools
features.description: Supports category-based and zero-based budgeting with rollover rules and spending trend views.

features.title: Shared budgets
features.description: Allows couples or households to share visibility into joint budgets and accounts.

features.title: Recurring bill tracking
features.description: Detects and tracks recurring transactions so users can anticipate upcoming bills.

features.title: Public API
features.description: Offers a documented API for developers to build custom automations or integrate Lunch Money data with other tools.

features.title: Pay-what-you-want pricing
features.description: Lets subscribers choose and lock in their own subscription price rather than being forced into fixed tiers.

pros: Pay-what-you-want pricing model lets long-time users lock in lower rates.
Strong multi-currency and cryptocurrency tracking not always found in mainstream budgeting apps.
Bootstrapped, founder-led company with a track record of responsive support.
Public API appeals to technically inclined users who want custom automations.
All subscribers get the full feature set, with no feature-gated tiers.
cons: No permanent free tier, only a free trial before payment is required.
Smaller company than venture-backed competitors like Monarch Money, which may mean fewer resources for rapid feature development.
Bank-sync reliability depends on third-party aggregation providers and can occasionally break.
Pay-what-you-want pricing can be confusing for users unsure what a fair price is.
No native investment advisory or robo-advisor features, unlike some finance apps.

faqs.question: Is Lunch Money free?
faqs.answer: Lunch Money offers a free trial, but after that it requires a paid, pay-what-you-want annual subscription; there is no permanent free tier.

faqs.question: What does 'pay-what-you-want' mean for Lunch Money pricing?
faqs.answer: Subscribers choose their own price above a minimum threshold, and that price is locked in for as long as they remain subscribed, rather than being assigned a fixed tier.

faqs.question: Does Lunch Money support multiple currencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lunch Money can track accounts and transactions in different currencies and converts them into a home currency for consolidated budgeting and reporting.

faqs.question: Can Lunch Money track cryptocurrency?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lunch Money supports tracking crypto holdings alongside traditional bank and investment accounts.

faqs.question: Who built Lunch Money?
faqs.answer: Lunch Money was founded by Jen Yip, who built the app after manually tracking her own multi-currency spending in a spreadsheet, and launched it publicly in 2019.

faqs.question: Does Lunch Money have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lunch Money provides a public API that developers can use to build custom integrations and automations.

use_cases.title: Multi-currency personal budgeting
use_cases.description: Travelers, expats, and digital nomads use Lunch Money to track spending across multiple currencies in one consolidated budget.

use_cases.title: Household budgeting
use_cases.description: Couples share a Lunch Money budget to track joint spending and savings goals together.

use_cases.title: Custom finance automation
use_cases.description: Developers and power users pull Lunch Money data via its API into spreadsheets or other tools for custom reporting.

pricing_plans.price: User-chosen, subject to a minimum; originally $29/year at launch
features: Price locked in for the life of the subscription

enrichment.short_description: Lusha is an Israeli B2B sales intelligence platform providing verified contact data, company insights, and prospecting tools through a browser extension, web app, and API.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Starter plan from about $37.45/month billed annually)
enrichment.headquarters: Tel Aviv, Israel, with additional US offices
enrichment.seo_title: Lusha Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lusha review 2026: B2B contact data and sales intelligence platform. See pricing tiers, credit system, key features, pros and cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lusha is a business-to-business sales intelligence and contact-data platform that helps sales, recruiting, and marketing teams find verified emails, phone numbers, and firmographic data for prospects. The company was founded in March 2016 in Tel Aviv, Israel, by Yoni Tserruya and Assaf Eisenstein, who built an early version of the product as a side project while Tserruya was working as an iOS developer. Lusha has grown into one of the more widely used sales-intelligence tools, competing directly with ZoomInfo, Apollo.io, Cognism, and Seamless.AI. Lusha has raised a total of roughly $245 million across its funding history, most notably a $205 million Series B round in 2021 that valued the company at approximately $1.5 billion, with investors including PSG Equity. The company reports tens of millions of dollars in annual recurring revenue and employs roughly 300 people between its Tel Aviv research and development hub and US offices. Architecturally, Lusha's core product is a crowd-sourced and verified contact database accessed primarily through a Chrome browser extension that overlays contact details on LinkedIn profiles and company websites, plus a standalone web app, CRM integrations such as Salesforce and HubSpot, and a REST API for programmatic enrichment. Lusha also offers Lusha Engage for outreach sequencing and Lusha Conversations for call intelligence, extending the product beyond pure contact-data lookup into a broader sales engagement suite. Lusha uses a freemium, credit-based pricing model. The free plan gives a single user 40 credits per month, enough for light prospecting, and includes the browser extension, CRM integrations, and basic access to Engage and Conversations. Paid plans consume credits faster for more valuable actions; for example, revealing an email typically costs fewer credits than revealing a phone number under the current rate structure. As of 2026, the self-serve paid tiers are Starter, Pro, and Premium, priced roughly from the high-30-dollar range to several hundred dollars per month per user when billed annually, with higher prices on monthly billing, while the top-tier Scale plan is custom-priced for larger sales organizations. Lusha's target audience is sales development reps, account executives, recruiters, and marketers who need to quickly find and verify contact information for outbound outreach, account-based marketing, or candidate sourcing. Its main differentiation versus larger incumbents like ZoomInfo is a lower entry price point, a lightweight browser-first workflow, and a crowd-sourced data model that blends user-contributed contact data with algorithmic verification, positioning it as a more accessible option for small and mid-sized sales teams rather than only large enterprises.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lusha is a B2B sales intelligence platform that provides verified email addresses, phone numbers, and company data to help sales, recruiting, and marketing teams find and reach prospects. It was founded in 2016 in Tel Aviv, Israel, by Yoni Tserruya and Assaf Eisenstein and has since raised roughly $245 million in funding, reaching a valuation of about $1.5 billion after its 2021 Series B round.

The company operates a crowd-sourced and verified contact database accessed mainly through a browser extension, alongside a web app, CRM integrations, and an API, and has expanded into sales engagement with its Engage and Conversations products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lusha's browser extension overlays verified contact details directly onto LinkedIn profiles and company websites, letting users reveal emails and phone numbers without leaving their existing workflow. The platform also provides company-level firmographic and intent data to help prioritize accounts.

Beyond contact lookup, Lusha includes Lusha Engage for building and running outreach sequences and Lusha Conversations for call intelligence, plus native integrations with CRMs like Salesforce and HubSpot and an API for bulk enrichment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lusha uses a freemium, credit-based model: the free plan gives one user 40 credits per month, while paid Starter, Pro, and Premium plans provide increasing monthly credit allowances, priced per user and cheaper when billed annually versus monthly.

Each contact reveal consumes credits, with emails costing fewer credits than phone numbers under the current rate structure. Larger sales organizations can move to the custom-priced Scale plan for higher volume and additional administrative controls.

features.title: Browser extension enrichment
features.description: Reveals verified emails and phone numbers directly on LinkedIn and company websites through a Chrome extension.

features.title: Verified contact database
features.description: Combines crowd-sourced contributions with verification processes to maintain accuracy of emails and direct-dial phone numbers.

features.title: Company and intent data
features.description: Provides firmographic details and buying-intent signals to help prioritize which accounts to target.

features.title: CRM integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, and other CRMs sync enriched contact data directly into existing sales workflows.

features.title: Lusha Engage
features.description: Built-in outreach sequencing tool for running multi-step email and call campaigns from within Lusha.

features.title: Lusha Conversations
features.description: Call intelligence features that record, transcribe, and analyze sales calls.

features.title: API access
features.description: REST API for bulk or programmatic contact and company enrichment integrated into custom tools.

features.title: Credit-based plans
features.description: Flexible per-user credit allowances scale from a free tier through custom enterprise pricing.

pros: Lower entry price than large incumbents like ZoomInfo, with a usable free tier.
Browser extension makes prospecting fast without leaving LinkedIn or a company website.
Includes outreach (Engage) and call intelligence (Conversations) beyond pure contact lookup.
Well-funded, established company with strong CRM integrations.
Credit system lets teams control spend by only paying for reveals they actually use.
cons: Credit-based pricing can get expensive quickly for teams doing high-volume phone-number lookups.
Phone-number reveal costs increased under recent pricing changes, raising effective per-contact cost.
Data coverage and accuracy can vary by region compared to email accuracy.
Enterprise-scale pricing on the Scale plan is not published and requires a custom quote.
Some users report the free plan's 40 monthly credits are consumed very quickly.

faqs.question: Is Lusha free to use?
faqs.answer: Lusha has a permanently free plan that includes 40 credits per month for a single user, along with the browser extension and basic CRM integrations.

faqs.question: How does Lusha's credit system work?
faqs.answer: Each contact reveal uses credits; revealing an email typically costs fewer credits than revealing a phone number, and paid plans include larger monthly credit allowances.

faqs.question: What is Lusha used for?
faqs.answer: Lusha is used primarily by sales, recruiting, and marketing teams to find and verify contact details for prospects and to run outreach campaigns.

faqs.question: Who founded Lusha?
faqs.answer: Lusha was founded in 2016 in Tel Aviv, Israel, by Yoni Tserruya and Assaf Eisenstein.

faqs.question: How much funding has Lusha raised?
faqs.answer: Lusha has raised approximately $245 million in total funding, including a $205 million Series B round in 2021 that valued the company at about $1.5 billion.

faqs.question: What are Lusha's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Lusha competes primarily with ZoomInfo, Apollo.io, Cognism, and Seamless.AI in the B2B sales intelligence space.

use_cases.title: Outbound sales prospecting

use_cases.audience: Sales development reps and account executives

use_cases.title: Recruiting and candidate sourcing
use_cases.description: Recruiters use Lusha to find direct contact details for passive candidates found on LinkedIn.

use_cases.description: Marketing teams use Lusha's company and intent data to prioritize target accounts and build enriched contact lists.
use_cases.audience: B2B marketing and demand generation teams

pricing_plans.price: About $37.45/month per user billed annually (about $49.90 billed monthly)
About $52.45/month per user billed annually (about $69.90 billed monthly)
About $299.95/month per user billed annually (about $399.90 billed monthly)

enrichment.short_description: Lyrion Music Server is a free, open-source, community-maintained media server, the successor to Logitech Media Server and Squeezebox Server, that streams personal music libraries to networked players.
enrichment.seo_title: Lyrion Music Server Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lyrion Music Server review 2026: the free, open-source successor to Logitech Media Server. Features, setup, pricing, pros and cons, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Lyrion Music Server (LMS) is a free, open-source media server for streaming personal digital music collections to a wide range of players, from dedicated Squeezebox hardware to third-party apps and smart speakers. Its history traces back to 2001, when Slim Devices released SlimServer alongside its SLIMP3 network audio player; the server software was later renamed SqueezeCenter and then Squeezebox Server as Slim Devices' hardware line evolved. Logitech acquired Slim Devices in 2006 and rebranded the server as Logitech Media Server while continuing to sell Squeezebox hardware players. Logitech discontinued Squeezebox hardware and stepped back from active development after version 7.7; from version 7.8 onward, starting March 2014, the software was maintained entirely by a volunteer community under the LMS-Community organization on GitHub, continuing through the 8.x release series. After Logitech shut down its associated mysqueezebox.com cloud service in March 2024, the community completed its separation from the Logitech brand by rebranding the project as Lyrion Music Server with the release of version 9.0 in November 2024, continuing the version numbering lineage from the 8.x series. Lyrion Music Server is written primarily in Perl and is licensed under the GNU General Public License. It runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, and BSD, and is commonly deployed on NAS devices from vendors like Synology and QNAP, as well as on minimal Linux distributions such as piCorePlayer built specifically for audio streaming. The server indexes a local music library and streams audio in formats including MP3, FLAC, WAV, Ogg Vorbis, Opus, and AAC to any number of connected players, supporting synchronized multi-room playback across grouped clients. Because it descends directly from Logitech Media Server, Lyrion Music Server remains compatible with the installed base of Squeezebox hardware players as well as newer software players like Squeezelite and third-party apps such as Material Skin, giving it a large library of client software despite the original hardware being long discontinued. A plugin architecture extends functionality to support internet radio, streaming services, and custom integrations. Because Lyrion is free, open-source, and self-hosted, it has no direct commercial competitors in the traditional sense; its closest comparisons are other self-hosted media and streaming servers such as Plex, Jellyfin, Navidrome, and Roon, a paid commercial alternative, with Lyrion differentiating itself through its long history with the Squeezebox ecosystem, its multi-room synchronized playback, and its fully community-governed, non-commercial development model. Its target audience is audiophiles and home-server hobbyists who already own or want to build networked audio systems and prefer self-hosting their music library over relying on streaming services alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lyrion Music Server is a free, open-source media server descended from Logitech Media Server, formerly SlimServer and Squeezebox Server, tracing back to Slim Devices' original SLIMP3 player software from 2001. Logitech acquired Slim Devices in 2006 and ran the project until stepping back after version 7.7, at which point a volunteer community took over development.

The project was rebranded from Logitech Media Server to Lyrion Music Server with version 9.0 in November 2024, following the shutdown of Logitech's mysqueezebox.com cloud service, marking a full and formal separation from the Logitech brand while preserving compatibility with the existing Squeezebox hardware and software ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Lyrion Music Server indexes a local digital music library and streams it to any number of connected players in formats including MP3, FLAC, WAV, Ogg Vorbis, Opus, and AAC, with support for synchronized, multi-room playback across grouped devices.

It runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, and BSD, and is popular on NAS devices from Synology and QNAP as well as minimal Linux images like piCorePlayer. A plugin architecture supports internet radio and additional streaming integrations, and it remains compatible with both original Squeezebox hardware and modern software players.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Lyrion Music Server is entirely free and open source, licensed under the GNU General Public License, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or licensing fees of any kind.

Because it is self-hosted, the only costs involved are for the hardware running the server, such as a home server, NAS, or Raspberry Pi, and any player hardware or software used to listen to the streamed music.

features.title: Multi-format streaming
features.description: Streams MP3, FLAC, WAV, Ogg Vorbis, Opus, and AAC files from a local library to any number of connected players.

features.title: Synchronized multi-room audio
features.description: Groups multiple players to play music in sync across different rooms.

features.title: Broad platform support
features.description: Runs on Linux, Windows, macOS, BSD, and popular NAS platforms like Synology and QNAP.

features.title: Squeezebox hardware compatibility
features.description: Continues to support the original Logitech Squeezebox hardware players alongside modern software clients.

features.title: Plugin architecture
features.description: Extends the server with community plugins for internet radio, streaming integrations, and custom features.

features.title: Web-based administration
features.description: Manages the music library, players, and settings through a browser-based interface.

features.title: Software player support
features.description: Works with third-party software players such as Squeezelite and apps like Material Skin.

features.title: Community-driven development
features.description: Actively maintained by the volunteer LMS-Community rather than a commercial vendor.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscriptions or licensing costs.
Long track record dating back over two decades, with a mature, stable codebase.
Strong multi-room, synchronized playback across many player types.
Continues to support legacy Squeezebox hardware that would otherwise be unsupported.
Runs on inexpensive hardware like NAS devices and Raspberry Pi.
cons: Interface and setup are more technical than commercial alternatives like Plex or Roon.
Relies entirely on volunteer maintainers, with no commercial support option.
Client and player ecosystem has shrunk since original Squeezebox hardware was discontinued.
Written in Perl, an aging language stack compared to some newer self-hosted media servers.
Less polished mobile app experience compared to modern competitors like Plex or Navidrome.

faqs.question: Is Lyrion Music Server free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Lyrion Music Server is entirely free and open source under the GNU General Public License, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What was Lyrion Music Server called before?
faqs.answer: It was previously known as Logitech Media Server, and before that SqueezeCenter and SlimServer, before the community rebranded it to Lyrion Music Server in November 2024 with version 9.0.

faqs.question: Do I need Squeezebox hardware to use Lyrion Music Server?
faqs.answer: No, Lyrion Music Server works with software players like Squeezelite and various mobile and desktop apps in addition to legacy Squeezebox hardware.

faqs.question: What audio formats does Lyrion Music Server support?
faqs.answer: It supports MP3, FLAC, WAV, Ogg Vorbis, Opus, and AAC, among other formats.

faqs.question: Who maintains Lyrion Music Server?
faqs.answer: It is maintained by the volunteer LMS-Community on GitHub, following Logitech's withdrawal from active development after version 7.7.

faqs.question: Can Lyrion Music Server run on a NAS?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is commonly installed on NAS devices from Synology and QNAP, as well as on minimal Linux distributions built for audio streaming.

use_cases.title: Whole-home audio streaming
use_cases.description: Audiophiles run Lyrion Music Server on a home server or NAS to stream a local music library to multiple synchronized rooms.

use_cases.title: Reviving legacy Squeezebox hardware
use_cases.description: Owners of older Squeezebox players use Lyrion Music Server to keep their hardware working after Logitech ended official support.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted music library management
use_cases.description: Users who prefer owning their music files over relying solely on streaming services self-host Lyrion to organize and stream their collection.

enrichment.short_description: m3ter is an enterprise usage-metering and rating platform that powers consumption-based billing for software companies, acquired by Salesforce in 2026 to power Agentforce Revenue Management.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing, typically six-figure annual enterprise contracts
enrichment.seo_title: m3ter Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: m3ter review 2026: enterprise usage-metering and billing platform now part of Salesforce. Features, pricing, pros and cons, and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: m3ter is an enterprise-grade usage-metering, rating, and pricing operations platform built to help software and SaaS companies run consumption-based and hybrid billing models at scale. It was founded in 2020 in London, United Kingdom, by Griffin Parry and John Griffin, who had previously co-founded GameSparks, a cloud backend services company for game developers that was acquired by Amazon Web Services in 2017. m3ter raised institutional funding to build out its platform, including a $17.5 million seed round backed by investors such as Union Square Ventures, Insight Partners, and Kindred Capital, followed by a $14 million Series A round led by Notion Capital in 2023, bringing its total funding to roughly $33 million before it was acquired. In June 2026, Salesforce signed a definitive agreement to acquire m3ter, with the deal closing on July 1, 2026. Salesforce integrated m3ter's high-volume mediation, metering, and rating engine into Agentforce Revenue Management, its billing and monetization layer for AI agent and consumption-based pricing, positioning m3ter's technology as core infrastructure for billing AI-agent usage and outcome-based pricing models across the Salesforce ecosystem. Architecturally, m3ter is built to ingest very high volumes of usage events in real time, apply complex, multi-dimensional pricing and rating logic across tiered, graduated, per-unit, or hybrid pricing spanning multiple metrics, and generate accurate, auditable bills that integrate downstream with invoicing, CRM, and revenue-recognition systems such as Salesforce, NetSuite, and Stripe Billing. Rather than being a full end-to-end billing and payments platform, m3ter has historically focused specifically on the metering and rating layer of the quote-to-cash process, the hardest part of usage-based billing to get right at scale, while relying on integrations for invoicing, tax, dunning, and payment collection. m3ter does not publish self-serve pricing; contracts are negotiated per customer and typically represent six-figure annual commitments, reflecting its target market of mid-market to large enterprise software companies undertaking a broader quote-to-cash transformation rather than small startups. Initial proof-of-concept implementations can be stood up in days, but full production rollouts as part of a broader billing overhaul commonly take three to six months. m3ter's primary competitors in the usage-based billing and metering space include Metronome, Amberflo, Togai, Orb, and larger billing platforms like Zuora, Chargebee, and BillingPlatform. Its differentiation has centered on deep metering and rating sophistication for complex, high-volume pricing models, now amplified by its integration into Salesforce's broader CRM and revenue-management ecosystem following the 2026 acquisition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: m3ter is an enterprise usage-metering and rating platform for software companies running consumption-based or hybrid pricing models. Founded in 2020 in London by Griffin Parry and John Griffin, former co-founders of GameSparks, which was acquired by Amazon in 2017, m3ter raised roughly $33 million from investors including Union Square Ventures, Insight Partners, and Notion Capital before being acquired.

In 2026, Salesforce signed a definitive agreement to acquire m3ter, closing the deal on July 1, 2026, and folding its metering and rating technology into Agentforce Revenue Management to support consumption- and outcome-based billing for AI agents and other usage-priced products across the Salesforce platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: m3ter is built to ingest usage events at high volume in real time and apply complex, multi-dimensional pricing logic, including tiered, graduated, and hybrid pricing across multiple usage metrics, then generate accurate, auditable bills.

Rather than covering the entire billing and payments stack, m3ter focuses specifically on the metering and rating layer, integrating with downstream invoicing, CRM, and revenue-recognition systems such as Salesforce, NetSuite, and Stripe Billing to complete the quote-to-cash process.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: m3ter does not publish self-serve pricing; all plans are custom-quoted for enterprise customers, and typical contracts represent six-figure annual commitments reflecting its focus on mid-market and enterprise software companies.

Initial proofs of concept can often be implemented within days, but full production deployments, usually part of a broader quote-to-cash transformation project, typically take three to six months to complete.

features.title: Real-time usage metering
features.description: Ingests high-volume usage events in real time to power accurate, up-to-date consumption tracking.

features.title: Multi-dimensional rating engine
features.description: Applies tiered, graduated, per-unit, and hybrid pricing logic across multiple usage metrics simultaneously.

features.title: Automated, auditable billing
features.description: Generates error-checked bills with a full audit trail to reduce billing disputes and revenue leakage.

features.title: Downstream integrations
features.description: Connects to invoicing, CRM, and revenue-recognition systems including Salesforce, NetSuite, and Stripe Billing.

features.title: Agentforce Revenue Management integration
features.description: Following its Salesforce acquisition, m3ter technology now underpins consumption and outcome-based billing for AI agents within Salesforce's platform.

features.title: Flexible pricing models
features.description: Supports usage-based, hybrid subscription-plus-usage, and outcome-based pricing structures.

features.title: Enterprise-scale architecture
features.description: Designed to handle very high transaction and event volumes for large software companies.

features.title: Rapid proof-of-concept setup
features.description: Can stand up initial pricing model proofs of concept within days ahead of a fuller rollout.

pros: Purpose-built, deep metering and rating engine for complex usage-based pricing models.
Now backed by Salesforce's resources and distribution following the 2026 acquisition.
Handles very high volumes of usage events with real-time processing.
Strong downstream integrations with major CRM, invoicing, and revenue-recognition systems.
Fast proof-of-concept timelines relative to full quote-to-cash transformations.
cons: No public, self-serve pricing; only suited to enterprises able to commit to custom, typically six-figure contracts.
Focuses narrowly on metering and rating rather than the full billing and payments stack, requiring additional tools for invoicing and collections.
Post-acquisition roadmap and standalone product direction are now tied to Salesforce's broader strategy.
Full implementations can take three to six months as part of larger transformation projects.
Not accessible to small startups or teams needing lightweight, self-serve usage billing.

faqs.question: What does m3ter do?
faqs.answer: m3ter is a usage-metering and rating platform that helps software companies process consumption data and generate accurate, auditable bills for usage-based and hybrid pricing models.

faqs.question: Who owns m3ter now?
faqs.answer: Salesforce acquired m3ter, signing a definitive agreement in June 2026 and closing the deal on July 1, 2026, integrating its technology into Agentforce Revenue Management.

faqs.question: How much does m3ter cost?
faqs.answer: m3ter does not publish pricing; it uses custom enterprise contracts that typically represent six-figure annual commitments.

faqs.question: Who founded m3ter?
faqs.answer: m3ter was founded in 2020 in London by Griffin Parry and John Griffin, who previously co-founded GameSparks, acquired by Amazon in 2017.

faqs.question: Is m3ter a full billing platform?
faqs.answer: No, m3ter focuses specifically on usage metering and rating, integrating with separate invoicing, payments, and revenue-recognition tools to complete the billing process.

faqs.question: What are m3ter's main competitors?
faqs.answer: m3ter competes with Metronome, Amberflo, Togai, Orb, and larger billing platforms like Zuora, Chargebee, and BillingPlatform.

use_cases.title: Consumption-based SaaS billing
use_cases.description: Software companies use m3ter to meter API calls, compute usage, or other consumption metrics and translate them into accurate invoices.

use_cases.title: Hybrid subscription-plus-usage pricing
use_cases.description: Companies combining flat subscription fees with usage overages use m3ter to manage the rating complexity.

enrichment.short_description: Maddy Mail Server is a free, open-source, all-in-one mail server written in Go that replaces Postfix, Dovecot, and related daemons with a single composable binary.
enrichment.seo_title: Maddy Mail Server Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Maddy Mail Server review 2026: a free, open-source, all-in-one Go mail server. Features, setup, licensing, pros and cons, and self-hosted alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Maddy Mail Server, often written as 'maddy,' is a free, open-source, self-hosted mail server written entirely in the Go programming language. It was created by developer Max Mazurov, known online as foxcpp, with the project's copyright dating from around 2019, and is maintained on GitHub as foxcpp/maddy with contributions from a community of dozens of contributors. Maddy's core design goal is to consolidate the many separate daemons traditionally required to run a full email server, an MTA like Postfix for sending mail, an MX or receiving component, an IMAP server like Dovecot for storage and retrieval, plus separate services for DKIM signing, SPF checking, DMARC enforcement, DANE, and MTA-STS, into a single binary with one unified configuration file. Functionally, Maddy can operate as a full mail transfer agent sending outbound mail via SMTP, as an MX server accepting inbound mail via SMTP, and as an IMAP server storing and serving mailboxes to mail clients, while natively implementing the modern email-authentication and security stack, DKIM, SPF, DMARC, DANE, and MTA-STS, that is increasingly required by major mail providers like Gmail and Outlook to avoid spam filtering. It supports flexible storage and delivery backends, including local filesystem storage, Amazon S3-compatible blob storage, local delivery queues, remote MX delivery, and SMTP/LMTP forwarding to downstream systems, along with authentication via PAM, system commands, or LDAP for integrating with existing user directories. Official Docker images are provided for containerized deployment, which is a common way Maddy is run in self-hosting communities. Maddy's license changed from the permissive MIT license to the copyleft GNU General Public License version 3 starting with version 0.3.3/0.4 and all subsequent releases, meaning any distributed modified versions must also be open-sourced under GPLv3 terms, a notable consideration for companies wanting to build proprietary products on top of it. As a fully open-source, self-hosted tool, Maddy has no commercial pricing; it is entirely free to download, configure, and run on a user's own server. Its target audience is technically proficient self-hosters, small businesses, privacy-focused individuals, and sysadmins who want to run their own email infrastructure without maintaining separately configured Postfix, Dovecot, OpenDKIM, and OpenSPF/OpenDMARC installations. Its main alternatives in the self-hosted mail server space include Postfix combined with Dovecot, Mailcow, Mail-in-a-Box, and Stalwart Mail Server, with Maddy differentiating itself through its single-binary, single-config-file simplicity and its modern Go codebase, at the cost of a smaller user base and fewer turnkey management interfaces than some of those alternatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Maddy Mail Server is a free, open-source, self-hosted mail server written in Go that consolidates the many components of a traditional email stack, an MTA, an MX server, an IMAP server, and separate DKIM, SPF, DMARC, DANE, and MTA-STS services, into one binary with a single configuration file. It was created by developer Max Mazurov (foxcpp), with the project dating from around 2019, and is maintained on GitHub with dozens of community contributors.

Maddy is aimed at technically capable self-hosters and small organizations who want to run their own email infrastructure without separately installing and configuring Postfix, Dovecot, OpenDKIM, OpenSPF, and OpenDMARC, trading some of the flexibility of those mature, individually specialized daemons for a simpler, unified setup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Maddy can send mail as an MTA, receive mail as an MX server, and store and serve mailboxes over IMAP, all from a single daemon. It natively implements the modern email authentication and security stack, DKIM, SPF, DMARC, DANE, and MTA-STS, that major providers increasingly require to keep outbound mail out of spam folders.

The server supports flexible storage backends including local filesystem and Amazon S3-compatible blob storage, multiple authentication methods including PAM, system commands, and LDAP, and official Docker images for containerized deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Maddy Mail Server is completely free and open source, with no paid tiers, licenses, or subscriptions. It is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3, having switched from the original MIT license starting with version 0.3.3/0.4.
Because it is self-hosted, the only real costs are the server or cloud instance running it; there is no vendor to pay and no usage-based fees.

features.title: All-in-one mail daemon
features.description: Combines MTA, MX, and IMAP functionality into a single Go binary instead of requiring separate Postfix and Dovecot installations.

features.title: Built-in email authentication
features.description: Natively implements DKIM, SPF, DMARC, DANE, and MTA-STS to keep outbound mail authenticated and out of spam filters.

features.title: Unified configuration
features.description: A single configuration file controls SMTP, IMAP, filtering, authentication, and local delivery rules.

features.title: Flexible storage backends
features.description: Supports local filesystem storage as well as Amazon S3-compatible blob storage for mailboxes.

features.title: Multiple authentication methods
features.description: Integrates with PAM, system commands, or LDAP for user authentication.

features.title: Docker support
features.description: Ships official Docker images for straightforward containerized deployment.

features.title: SMTP/LMTP forwarding
features.description: Supports remote MX delivery and forwarding to downstream mail systems.

features.title: Lightweight Go codebase
features.description: Written entirely in Go, making it lightweight, fast, and relatively easy to audit compared to older C-based mail daemons.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing costs.
Replaces a stack of separate daemons, including Postfix, Dovecot, OpenDKIM, OpenSPF, and OpenDMARC, with one binary and one config file.
Modern Go codebase that is easier to audit and deploy than legacy C-based mail servers.
Native support for the full modern email security stack, including DANE and MTA-STS.
Official Docker images simplify self-hosted deployment.
cons: GPLv3 license requires derivative distributed works to also be open source, which can be a constraint for commercial products.
Smaller community and ecosystem than long-established projects like Postfix and Dovecot.
Fewer turnkey management interfaces or admin panels compared to bundled solutions like Mailcow.
Running any self-hosted mail server, including Maddy, requires ongoing sysadmin effort for deliverability and security.
As a newer project, it has a smaller track record in large-scale production deployments compared to decades-old mail server software.

faqs.question: Is Maddy Mail Server free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Maddy is completely free and open source, licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3.

faqs.question: What does Maddy replace?
faqs.answer: Maddy is designed to replace the combination of Postfix, Dovecot, OpenDKIM, OpenSPF, and OpenDMARC with a single unified mail server daemon.

faqs.question: What language is Maddy written in?
faqs.answer: Maddy is written entirely in the Go programming language.

faqs.question: Does Maddy support DKIM, SPF, and DMARC?
faqs.answer: Yes, Maddy natively implements DKIM, SPF, DMARC, DANE, and MTA-STS for modern email authentication and security.

faqs.question: Who created Maddy Mail Server?
faqs.answer: Maddy was created by developer Max Mazurov, known online as foxcpp, and is maintained as an open-source project on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can Maddy run in Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, Maddy provides official Docker images for containerized deployment.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted email for small organizations
use_cases.description: Small businesses and organizations run Maddy to host their own email without relying on a third-party provider.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused personal email
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious individuals self-host Maddy to keep full control over their own mailboxes and data.

use_cases.title: Simplified mail infrastructure for developers
use_cases.description: Developers who want a single, easy-to-configure mail daemon instead of managing Postfix and Dovecot separately choose Maddy for test and production environments.

features: Full MTA, MX, and IMAP functionality
Built-in DKIM, SPF, DMARC, DANE, and MTA-STS

enrichment.short_description: Mage, also known as Mage.ai, is an open-source data pipeline tool for building, running, and orchestrating ETL/ELT workflows in Python, SQL, and R, with a paid Mage Pro cloud and enterprise tier.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source, MIT license); Mage Pro Starter from about $100/month plus compute usage
enrichment.seo_title: Mage (Mage.ai) Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mage.ai review 2026: open-source data pipeline and orchestration tool. See Mage Pro pricing tiers, features, pros and cons, and top ETL alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mage, commercially branded Mage.ai, is an open-source data pipeline and orchestration tool for building, running, and managing ETL/ELT workflows that integrate and transform data. It was created by Mage Technologies, Inc., a startup founded in 2020 and headquartered in Santa Clara, California, by co-founders Tommy Dang and Xiaoyou Wang. The company has raised approximately $11.8 million across two seed rounds led by Gradient Ventures, Google's AI-focused venture fund, with an initial $6.3 million round in October 2021 joined by Designer Fund and Alumni Ventures, followed by a $5.49 million extension in March 2023. Mage's founding premise is that data teams waste significant time stitching together separate tools for data ingestion, transformation, and orchestration, and that a single open-source platform that unifies these functions can reduce both the manual coding burden and deployment complexity that comes with assembling a custom stack from tools like Airflow, dbt, and Fivetran. The open-source core, released under the MIT license, lets users build real-time and batch data pipelines using Python, SQL, and R inside a notebook-like development interface, with built-in orchestration, scheduling, versioning, and observability rather than requiring these to be bolted on separately. Beyond the free, self-hosted open-source edition, Mage offers a commercial product called Mage Pro, a managed cloud and enterprise platform with tiered plans: a Starter plan around $100/month plus $0.29 per compute hour, followed by Team, Plus, Business, and Enterprise tiers that scale up to roughly $25,000/month for the largest organizations, each adding more collaboration, governance, and support capabilities on top of the same compute-hour billing model. Mage Pro can be deployed as a fully managed cloud service, in a hybrid configuration where the control plane runs in Mage's cloud but data processing stays in the customer's private cloud, or fully within a customer's private cloud for maximum data residency and security control, with regional availability across the US, Canada, Europe, Asia, and Australia. Mage's target audience spans individual data engineers and analytics engineers using the free open-source tool, up to data platform teams at larger companies adopting Mage Pro for governed, collaborative pipeline development. Its primary competitors include Apache Airflow, Dagster, Prefect, and dbt in the orchestration and transformation space, as well as newer AI-oriented data pipeline tools; Mage differentiates itself through its notebook-style, low-friction developer experience, unified support for Python, SQL, and R in a single pipeline, and a clear path from free open-source usage to a managed commercial product without switching tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mage, or Mage.ai, is an open-source data pipeline and orchestration tool for building, running, and managing ETL/ELT workflows that integrate and transform data. It was founded in 2020 by Tommy Dang and Xiaoyou Wang and is headquartered in Santa Clara, California, having raised roughly $11.8 million in seed funding led by Google's Gradient Ventures.

The project's core premise is consolidating data ingestion, transformation, and orchestration, traditionally handled by separate tools like Airflow, dbt, and Fivetran, into a single open-source platform with a notebook-style development experience, reducing both custom glue code and deployment complexity for data teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mage lets users build real-time and batch data pipelines using Python, SQL, and R within an interactive, notebook-like interface, with built-in scheduling, orchestration, versioning, and pipeline observability rather than requiring separate tools bolted together.

The commercial Mage Pro product extends the open-source core with managed cloud hosting, hybrid and private-cloud deployment options, team collaboration and governance features, and regional deployment availability across the US, Canada, Europe, Asia, and Australia.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mage's open-source core is free and MIT-licensed, and can be self-hosted at no cost. Mage Pro, the managed commercial product, starts with a Starter plan at around $100/month plus $0.29 per compute hour, moving up through Team, Plus, and Business tiers.
At the top end, Mage Pro's Enterprise plan runs around $25,000/month, with every paid tier billed on top of a base compute-hour rate that resets monthly along with block runs and AI token usage.

features.title: Notebook-style pipeline builder
features.description: Build real-time and batch data pipelines using Python, SQL, and R inside an interactive, notebook-like development environment.

features.title: Built-in orchestration and scheduling
features.description: Native scheduling and orchestration remove the need to bolt on a separate workflow orchestrator like Airflow.

features.title: Pipeline versioning and observability
features.description: Tracks pipeline changes and surfaces run history and monitoring without extra tooling.

features.title: Open-source, self-hosted core
features.description: The MIT-licensed open-source edition can be deployed on a user's own infrastructure at no cost.

features.title: Managed cloud (Mage Pro)
features.description: A fully managed cloud deployment removes infrastructure setup, maintenance, and update burden.

features.title: Hybrid and private-cloud deployment
features.description: Enterprise customers can keep data processing inside their own private cloud while Mage manages the control plane, or run the entire platform privately.

features.title: Multi-region availability
features.description: Regional deployment options span the US, Canada, Europe, Asia, and Australia.

features.title: Compute-based billing
features.description: Paid Mage Pro tiers combine a base subscription fee with per-compute-hour usage charges.

pros: Free, MIT-licensed open-source core with no cost to self-host.
Unified support for Python, SQL, and R in a single pipeline, reducing tool sprawl.
Notebook-style interface lowers the learning curve compared to code-only orchestrators like Airflow.
Clear upgrade path from free open source to a managed commercial product, Mage Pro, without switching tools.
Flexible deployment options, including private-cloud and hybrid configurations for enterprises with data residency requirements.
cons: Mage Pro pricing combines a base subscription with per-compute-hour charges, which can be harder to predict than flat-rate pricing.
Smaller ecosystem and community than long-established orchestrators like Apache Airflow.
Enterprise tier pricing, around $25,000/month, is a significant commitment for large organizations.
As a younger, venture-backed company, long-term roadmap and support depend on continued funding or revenue growth.
Notebook-style pipelines may be less familiar to teams standardized on pure code-based orchestration like Airflow DAGs.

faqs.question: Is Mage.ai free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mage's open-source core is free and MIT-licensed and can be self-hosted at no cost; the paid Mage Pro product adds managed cloud hosting and enterprise features.

faqs.question: What languages does Mage support for pipelines?
faqs.answer: Mage pipelines can be built using Python, SQL, and R within its notebook-style interface.

faqs.question: How much does Mage Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Mage Pro starts at around $100/month for the Starter plan plus $0.29 per compute hour, with Team, Plus, Business, and Enterprise tiers scaling up to roughly $25,000/month.

faqs.question: Who founded Mage.ai?
faqs.answer: Mage was founded in 2020 by Tommy Dang and Xiaoyou Wang and is headquartered in Santa Clara, California.

faqs.question: Can Mage be deployed in a private cloud?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mage Pro supports hybrid deployment, where the control plane runs in Mage's cloud but data processing stays in the customer's private cloud, as well as fully private-cloud deployment.

faqs.question: What are Mage's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Mage competes with Apache Airflow, Dagster, Prefect, and dbt in the data pipeline and orchestration space.

use_cases.title: ETL/ELT pipeline development
use_cases.description: Data engineers use Mage to build and orchestrate pipelines that extract, transform, and load data between systems.

use_cases.title: Analytics engineering workflows
use_cases.description: Analytics teams use Mage's SQL and Python support to build transformation pipelines feeding dashboards and reports.

use_cases.title: Enterprise data platform governance
use_cases.description: Larger organizations adopt Mage Pro's hybrid or private-cloud deployment to run governed, collaborative pipelines while keeping data within their own infrastructure.

enrichment.short_description: Magento Open Source is Adobe's free, self-hosted, open-source e-commerce platform providing the core Magento commerce engine without Adobe Commerce's paid enterprise features.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted; hosting, development, and extensions cost extra)
enrichment.headquarters: San Jose, California, United States (Adobe headquarters; Magento was originally developed by Varien in Culver City, California)
enrichment.seo_title: Magento Open Source Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Magento Open Source review 2026: Adobe's free, self-hosted e-commerce platform. Features, real costs, pros and cons, and alternatives like Shopify.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Magento Open Source is the free, self-hosted, community-oriented edition of the Magento e-commerce platform, now owned and maintained by Adobe. The underlying Magento platform was originally developed by Varien Inc., a private company based in Culver City, California, with the first general-availability release published on March 31, 2008 after development began in 2007. eBay acquired a 49 percent stake in Magento for $90 million in March 2010 and bought out the remaining stake roughly a year later, folding it into eBay Enterprise before the platform was later spun out and sold to private equity firm Permira in 2015. Adobe then acquired Magento Commerce for $1.68 billion, completing the deal on June 19, 2018, and has operated it since as part of Adobe Experience Cloud alongside its paid Adobe Commerce product. Magento Open Source contains the core Magento commerce engine: catalog and inventory management, an integrated checkout, payment and shipping workflow, multi-store and multi-currency support, a WYSIWYG page builder, mobile-optimized storefronts, and access to the Adobe Commerce (Magento) Marketplace for extensions and themes. It is built on PHP and MySQL/MariaDB, uses a modular architecture, and requires the merchant to provide and manage their own hosting, unlike Adobe Commerce Cloud, which includes managed cloud infrastructure. Critically, Magento Open Source excludes the advanced capabilities reserved for the paid Adobe Commerce tier, such as built-in B2B functionality, AI-driven personalization powered by Adobe Sensei, managed cloud hosting on AWS, dedicated 24/7 Adobe support, and a contractual 99.99 percent uptime SLA. While the Magento Open Source software license itself carries no fee, Adobe Commerce licensing starts at roughly $22,000 per year for merchants with $1 to $5 million in gross merchandise value and scales past $125,000 per year for larger merchants, illustrating the gap between the free and paid tiers. Even for Magento Open Source, total cost of ownership is significant once hosting, custom development, extensions, and ongoing maintenance are factored in, with realistic all-in annual costs commonly estimated between $122,000 and $450,000 or more for a full implementation, since a free license does not mean a low-cost project. Magento Open Source's target audience is mid-sized and larger merchants with development resources who need deep customizability and control over their commerce platform and are willing to manage their own hosting and technical stack, as opposed to smaller merchants better served by hosted platforms. Its main competitors and alternatives include Shopify Plus, WooCommerce, BigCommerce, Adobe's own Adobe Commerce as an upgrade path, and Shopware, with Magento Open Source differentiating itself through deep extensibility, a large existing global ecosystem of Magento developers and agencies, and a clear upgrade path to Adobe Commerce as merchants scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Magento Open Source is Adobe's free, self-hosted edition of the Magento e-commerce platform, containing the core commerce engine without the advanced features reserved for the paid Adobe Commerce tier. Magento was originally built by Varien Inc., launching its first general-availability release in March 2008, before eBay acquired it in stages between 2010 and 2011 and Adobe ultimately bought Magento Commerce for $1.68 billion in June 2018.

Today Magento Open Source sits alongside Adobe Commerce as the free, community-oriented entry point into the Magento ecosystem, aimed at merchants and development teams who want full control over customization and hosting rather than a fully managed, paid enterprise platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Magento Open Source includes catalog and inventory management, an integrated checkout, payment and shipping workflow, multi-store and multi-currency support, a WYSIWYG page builder, and mobile-optimized storefronts, all built on a modular PHP and MySQL/MariaDB architecture that merchants self-host.

It provides access to the Adobe Commerce (Magento) Marketplace for extensions and themes, letting merchants extend functionality, but excludes Adobe Commerce-only capabilities such as built-in B2B tools, Adobe Sensei-powered AI personalization, managed AWS cloud hosting, dedicated 24/7 support, and a contractual uptime SLA.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Magento Open Source itself carries no license fee and is free to download and use. However, merchants are responsible for their own hosting, security, performance tuning, extensions, and ongoing development, so realistic total costs of ownership can run well into six figures annually for a full implementation.

Merchants who outgrow Magento Open Source's self-managed model can upgrade to Adobe Commerce, which starts at roughly $22,000 per year for smaller merchants and scales past $125,000 per year for larger ones, adding managed hosting, B2B features, AI personalization, and enterprise-grade support.

features.title: Catalog and inventory management
features.description: Manages complex product catalogs, variants, and inventory across single or multiple stores.

features.title: Integrated checkout and payments
features.description: Built-in checkout flow with support for numerous payment and shipping integrations.

features.title: Multi-store and multi-currency support
features.description: Runs multiple storefronts and currencies from a single Magento Open Source installation.

features.title: WYSIWYG page builder
features.description: Lets merchandisers build and edit storefront pages visually without writing code.

features.title: Marketplace extensions
features.description: Access to the Adobe Commerce (Magento) Marketplace for third-party extensions and themes.

features.title: Mobile-optimized storefronts
features.description: Responsive, mobile-friendly storefront templates out of the box.

features.title: Modular PHP architecture
features.description: Built on PHP and MySQL/MariaDB with a modular codebase that developers can extensively customize.

features.title: Global selling support
features.description: Supports selling to international markets with localization and multi-currency capabilities.

pros: No license fee, giving merchants full source-code access and control at zero software cost.
Deep extensibility and customization through a modular architecture and large extension marketplace.
Large, established global ecosystem of Magento developers and specialized agencies.
Clear upgrade path to Adobe Commerce for merchants that later need enterprise features or managed hosting.
Backed by Adobe, giving the platform long-term corporate stability compared to smaller open-source e-commerce projects.
cons: Requires merchants to source, secure, and manage their own hosting, unlike managed platforms such as Shopify.
Total cost of ownership, including hosting, development, extensions, and maintenance, can be substantial despite the free license.
Lacks Adobe Commerce's built-in B2B features, AI personalization, and enterprise SLA.
Steeper technical learning curve than hosted platforms like Shopify or BigCommerce.
Requires ongoing developer resources for updates, security patches, and performance optimization.

faqs.question: Is Magento Open Source really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Magento Open Source software license is free, but merchants must pay separately for hosting, development, extensions, and ongoing maintenance.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Magento Open Source and Adobe Commerce?
faqs.answer: Magento Open Source is the free, self-hosted core platform, while Adobe Commerce is Adobe's paid enterprise tier that adds managed cloud hosting, built-in B2B features, AI personalization via Adobe Sensei, dedicated support, and an uptime SLA.

faqs.question: Who owns Magento now?
faqs.answer: Adobe has owned Magento since completing its $1.68 billion acquisition of Magento Commerce in June 2018.

faqs.question: When was Magento founded?
faqs.answer: Magento's first general-availability release was published in March 2008 by Varien Inc., after development began in 2007.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to run a Magento Open Source store?
faqs.answer: While the software license is free, realistic total costs including hosting, development, extensions, and maintenance commonly range from roughly $122,000 to $450,000 or more per year for a full implementation.

faqs.question: What are the alternatives to Magento Open Source?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Shopify Plus, WooCommerce, BigCommerce, Shopware, and Adobe's own paid Adobe Commerce tier.

use_cases.title: Custom mid-market e-commerce
use_cases.description: Mid-sized merchants with in-house or agency development resources use Magento Open Source to build highly customized storefronts.

use_cases.title: Multi-store international retail
use_cases.description: Merchants selling across multiple countries and currencies use Magento Open Source's multi-store architecture to manage several storefronts from one installation.

use_cases.title: Agency-built e-commerce projects
use_cases.description: Development agencies build and maintain client stores on Magento Open Source, leveraging its large extension ecosystem and developer talent pool.
